title: The Reduction modulo p of Crystalline Breuil—Kisin Modules
speaker: Toby Gee
abstract: I will talk about (very much in progress) joint work with Mark Kisin on a Hodge—Newton style inequality for the mod p Breuil—Kisin modules arising from crystalline Galois representations.

proof_stages.label: Crystalline reduction problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes a crystalline p-adic Galois representation and asks what can be said about its mod-p reduction from its Hodge--Tate weights, with modular-form congruences as motivation.

proof_stages.label: Breuil--Kisin module avatar
proof_stages.summary: A crystalline lattice is replaced by its Breuil--Kisin module, a finite-height Frobenius module over a power-series ring whose mod-p reduction retains refined information about the restricted Galois representation.

proof_stages.label: Hodge type and Bruhat dominance
proof_stages.summary: The Hodge type of a mod-p Kisin module is defined by elementary divisors of the linearized Frobenius, and the known Levin-type result says it is bounded above by the Hodge--Tate weights in Bruhat order.

proof_stages.label: Modulo-p congruence theorem
proof_stages.summary: The main new theorem states that the Hodge type of the mod-p Breuil--Kisin module is congruent, as a multiset modulo p, to the Hodge--Tate weights, with expected analogues for split reductive groups.

proof_stages.label: Consequences, semisimplification, and scope
proof_stages.summary: Combining dominance and congruence gives predicted finite lists of possible reductions, survives passage to semisimplification, agrees with several GL_2 and GL_3 checks, and also reveals limitations over fields with multiple embeddings.

proof_stages.label: Filtered Kisin-module reduction
proof_stages.summary: The proof defines the Hodge filtration on the Frobenius pullback of the Kisin module, compares its reduction with the saturated mod-p filtration, and translates the two Hodge multisets into weight functions of graded pieces.

proof_stages.label: F-gauge and Weyl-algebra cone
proof_stages.summary: The comparison between the reduced F-gauge and its saturation has a cone supported at the mod-p point, and its structure as a graded Weyl-algebra module forces no change after summing weights in each residue class modulo p.

examples.name: One-dimensional crystalline characters over Q_p
examples.note: The character lambda_alpha epsilon^i and the rank-one Kisin module with Frobenius alpha u^i make concrete that Galois reduction only sees i modulo p-1 while the mod-p Kisin module retains the integer i.
examples.engagement_reason: He writes a one-dimensional crystalline character as an unramified twist times a cyclotomic power, computes its mod-p reduction, and later computes the corresponding rank-one Breuil--Kisin module.

examples.name: Two-dimensional crystalline representations with Hodge weights {0,h}
examples.note: The GL_2/Q_p family, parametrized by a_p, is used to move from explicit reduction formulas to the idea that the possible inertial exponents may be governed by simple congruence data from the Hodge--Tate weights.
examples.engagement_reason: He specializes to two-dimensional crystalline representations with weights {0,h}, analyzes the AP parameter and inertia cases, and derives the allowed Hodge-type possibilities in the range p+1 to 2p.

passes.reason: This is a standard two-dimensional crystalline family normalized by twist, used as the main test case rather than to refute a claim.

examples.name: Breuil's h=2p explicit formula
examples.note: The condition involving v_p(a_p^2+p) and an unramified trace polynomial illustrates how quickly exact formulas for a fixed crystalline representation become hard to guess.
examples.engagement_reason: He discusses Breuil’s explicit h=2p case by checking the relevant inertia exponent and stating the valuation condition and unramified-polynomial trace formula.

examples.name: Modular-form congruences from Sym^{k-2} Jordan--Holder factors
examples.note: The comparison with mod-p modular forms and representations of GL_2(F_p) serves as an external check that the predicted upper bounds match known lower bounds in the GL_2 case.
examples.engagement_reason: He traces the modular-form congruence mechanism via Sym^{k-2} Jordan--Holder factors, explaining how it produces higher-weight congruences and lower bounds on reductions.

examples.name: Unequal Hodge weights over Q_{p^2}
examples.note: The two-embedding weight pattern with very different sizes shows that the congruence theorem need not give a tight upper bound over unramified quadratic extensions.
examples.engagement_reason: He identifies a concrete Q_{p^2} unequal-weight pattern, explains why the bound fails there, and sketches applying Breuil--Mézard/Jordan--Holder calculations to see that not all predicted possibilities occur.

passes.reason: The highly unbalanced Hodge weights over the two embeddings give an extreme case used to show the proposed upper bound is not tight over Q_{p^2}.
